content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To     : Members of the Commission                    Date : November 25, 1977


From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
       Barbara Campbell

Subject: Reporting of Trust Holdings under the Political Reform Act

     The purpose of this memorandum is to outline the current
reporting requirements of trust holdings under the Political Reform
Act ("Act") and to solicit ideas on whether certain types of trusts
or interests should be excluded from reporting. Since the Act refers
to business trust and trusts, I will begin with a description of the
difference between them. I will then describe the present reporting
requirements for trusts and trust holdings under the Act.

     In the usual trust situation a maker of a trust places some
assets under the control of a trustee who has legal title to the
assets. The trustee manages the trust property and distributes the
income from that property to beneficiaries as directed in the trust.
The maker of the trust may also be a beneficiary of the trust.

     Initially, a distinction must be made between a business
trust and an ordinary trust since they are treated differently for
disclosure purposes under the Act. A business trust,1/ also known as
a "Massachusetts Trust," is a means of carrying on profit making
activity. The principal distinction between a business trust and an
ordinary trust is found in the purpose. The purpose of a business
trust is to carry on a business enterprise for profit whereas the
purpose of an ordinary trust is to conserve the property of the trust
with incidental powers of sale and investment. This distinction is
elaborated on in Treasury Regulation Section 301.7701-4(b):

     There are other arrangements which are known as trusts
because the legal title to property is converted to trustees
for the benefits of beneficiaries, but which are not classi-
fied as trusts for purposes of the Internal Revenue Code
because they are not simply arrangements to protect or
conserve the property for beneficiaries. These trusts,
which are often known as business or commercial trusts,
generally are created by the beneficiaries simply as

1/  The California Supreme Court recognized the use of the
business trust in Goldwater v. Oltman, 210 Cal. 408 (1930).

Members of the Commission
November 25, 1977
Page Two


a device to carry on a profitmaking business which normally
would have been carried on through business organizations
that are classified as corporations or partnerships under
the Internal Revenue Code....

In Morrissey v. Commissioner, 296 U.S. 344, 360 (1935), the
United States Supreme Court held the attributes of a business trust
made it "sufficiently analogous to corporate organization to justify
the conclusion that Congress intended that the income of the enterprise should be taxed in the same manner as that of corporations."
In Morrissey, the Court outlined the factors which make certain trusts
analogous to a corporate organization. The factors outlined in Treasury
Regulations Section 301.7701-2 and -3 and include: the number of
persons involved in creating the trust; an objective to carry on a
business and divide its gain; continuity of life; centralization of
management; limited liability and free transferability of interests.
The presence of the first two characteristics will usually distinguish a business trust from an ordinary trust. The rule adopted by
the Supreme Court in Morrissey and by Internal Revenue Service is
that if the character of a trust more nearly resembles a corporation
than a trust, it will be taxed as a corporation.

Therefore the Act's specific inclusion of business trust
within the definition of business entity in Government Code Section
82005 2/ was appropriate since business entity is defined as "any
organization or enterprise operated for profit." As demonstrated
above, a business trust is operated for profit.

However, an ordinary trust is not set up strictly for profit.
It is established primarily for the conservation and management of
assets. A trust also allows for the protection of beneficiaries and
for certain estate, income and inheritance tax advantages. Generally
the trustee of the trust makes the investment decisions. The beneficiary of a trust is not involved in the management of the trust but
is a passive recipient of the benefits, either income or principal,
of the trust.

In California, all trusts are revocable unless expressly
stated to be irrevocable, California Civil Code Section 2280. At
present there is no distinction between an irrevocable and revocable
trust for disclosure purposes. Generally the irrevocable or revocable
nature of a trust does not affect the required disclosure by a beneficiary under a trust. However in the case of a revocable trust, the
maker of the trust may have an interest in the trust which is disclosable since the trust could be revoked and the trust's assets returned
to the possession and control of the person who created the trust in
the first instance.

2/ All references will be to the Government Code unless
otherwise stated.

Members of the Commission
November 25, 1977
Page Three


Turning to the reporting requirements, the Act requires
disclosure of certain investments/ with a fair market value of more
than $1,000. Investment is defined as any financial interest in or
security issued by a business entity. Section 82034. The definition
of a "business entity" includes "business trusts." Section 82005.
Accordingly, if an individual/ had an interest in a business entity,
including a business trust, of $1,000 or more, that investment in the
business entity would be disclosed on the filer's disclosure state-
ment. However, there is no requirement in the Act that an interest
in an ordinary, non-business trust be reported as an investment.

The Act also requires disclosure of a person's pro rata
share of the income,2/ interests in real property or investments held
by a business entity or trust where the person or spouse has a ten
percent or greater interest, direct, indirect or beneficial, in the
business entity or trust. To be reportable, the ten percent or greater
pro rata share must be greater than $1,000 for interests in real
property or investments and greater than $250 for income. Sections
82030, 82033-34.

There are two modifications to this general pro rata rule.
As stated earlier, in reporting investments or interests in real
property, the investments and real property interests of the spouse
and dependent children are also reported. Section 82029.

The second modification to the pro rata reporting relates
to a business entity in which an individual holds a 50 percent or
greater interest. Under Section 87206(e), when an individual holds a
50 percent or greater interest in a business entity, all of the in-
vestments and/or interests in real property held by the business

_______________________
3/ Disclosure is only required if the business entity or
parent, subsidiary or otherwise related business entity has an interest
in real property in the jurisdiction or does business or plans to do
business in the jurisdiction or has done business in the jurisdiction
in the last two years.

4/ Section 82029 requires that in disclosing investments
or interests in real property, the investments or interests in real
property of the immediate family, defined as spouse and dependent
children, shall also be disclosed.

5/ Income is defined in Section 82030(b) so as to exclude
gifts from certain relatives, devises or inheritances, interest and
dividends and interest or return on a security registered with the
Securities and Exchange Commission.

Members of the Commission
November 25, 1977
Page Four


entity must be disclosed. As an example, if a filer has a 60 percent
interest in XYZ, a business trust, the filer would disclose all of
the investments held by XYZ, not just the filer's 60 percent pro data
share. However, if the official had a 60 percent interest in ABC, a
non-business trust, the filer would only be required to report his 60
percent pro rata share of the investments held by the ABC trust.

To apply the present reporting, then, if a filer or spouse
has a ten percent interest or greater in a non-business trust which
earns income or holds investments or interests in real property, the
filer's pro rata share of the income earned by the trust and investments
or interests in real property held by the trust must be reported to
the extent the pro rata share of the income exceeds $250 and to the
extent the pro rata share of investments or interests in real property
exceed $1,000. For example, if an individual had a 20 percent interest
in a trust holding investments, the pro rata share of any investment
of the trust of $5,000 or more would have to be disclosed if it met
the requirements of Section 82034. Under Section 87206(a) and (b),
the name of the business entity in which the investment is held and a
general description of the business activity would have to be disclosed
as well as the filer's pro rata share of the investment held by the
trust. This requirement was stated by the Commission in the Opinion
Requested by Vincent F Blondo, 1 PPPC Ops. 54 (75-036, July 2, 1975).
In that opinion the Commission stated "Since under Section 82034 the
Mayor's pro rata share of the trust's stock constitutes an investment
of the Mayor, the ownership by the trust of San Diego Gas and Electric
Company shares must be listed and a general description of the Company's
business activities stated."

If an individual held a 40 percent interest in a non-business
trust which consisted of one investment valued at $100,000, the indi-
vidual would report and identify that investment of $40,000.6/

It has been suggested that if a filer has a ten percent
interest in ABC trust, a non-business trust which has investments, the
filer would only have to disclose "ten percent interest in ABC trust."
That suggestion ignores the language of Sections 82030, 82033 and
82034 requiring reporting of the pro rata share of the income, interests
in real property or investments held by the trust and does not result
in meaningful disclosure.

_______________________
6/ The amount of the individual's pro rata share would not
be reported. The appropriate box indicating the value as greater
than $10,000 but less than $100,000 would be marked.

Members of the Commission
November 25, 1977
Page Five


     The Act does not require disclosure of the pro rata share
of the income, investments or interests in real property of all
trusts. It only requires disclosure of the pro rata share when an
individual has a ten percent or greater interest which has a value
of $1,000 or more for interests in real property and/or investments
and $250 for income. The holdings of the trust could be affected
and influenced by the public official's actions and should be disclosed.

     The staff has given informal advice that in determining
the ownership interest in a trust, the life estate and remainder
interests will be considered separately. A life estate is essential-
ly a right to receive the income from a trust during one's lifetime.
A remainder interest is a right to receive the assets of a trust
once the trust is terminated. If a filer has a 50 percent life
estate in a trust which has investments, the filer will have to
disclose her pro rata share of all investments of the trust to the
extent that the pro rata share exceeds $1,000. However, if the
filer did not have a life estate but three dependent children had
remainder interests in a trust established by a grandparent, the
filer would have a 100 percent interest in the trust because the
three children had 100 percent of the remainder interest.

     The preceding described the reporting requirements for
trusts under the Act. The staff would like to receive direction
from the Commissioners on whether any of those requirements should
be changed. There are several additional areas in which the staff
would like direction.

     First, there is no definition 8/ of what is a direct, indirect
or beneficial interest in a trust. The staff believes a life estate

_________
     7/ If the spouse and filer each had a 50 percent interest,
the filer would have a 100 percent interest in the investments and
interests in real property, to the extent they were greater than $1,000.
But in the case for income, it would be different. The staff advised,
in the letter of advice to Frank Zolin, Executive Officer of Los Angeles
Superior Court, dated September 15, 1976, that in reporting income
only the filer's separate property interest would be reported. Section
8707 requires a filer to report his community property interest in
income received by the filer's spouse, which means the threshold for
income of a trust would be $500 (instead of $250). Therefore if the
spouse's 50 percent interest was his separate property, the filer
would only report her 50 percent share of income from the trust.

     8/ As a possibility, the Commission could adopt the standard
described in Section 87103(d) which defines an indirect investment or
interest as "any investment or interest owned ... by a trust in which
he has a substantial interest.... A public official has a substantial
interest in a trust when the official, his spouse and dependent children
have a present or future interest worth more than one thousand dollars
($1,000)." However, since the present or future interest is in no way
defined, the remoteness concern would not be addressed.

Members of the Commission
November 25, 1977
Page six


interest in a trust should be disclosed, when the pro rata share of
the trust is ten percent or greater and meets the monetary thresholds.
If a trust is revocable, the maker of the trust has an interest which
should be disclosed since he or she can gain control over the trust
assets simply by revoking the trust. If a trust is irrevocable, any
life estate held by a filer or spouse would be a disclosable interest.
The Commission may want to define whether some remote interests in a
trust would not be considered direct, indirect or beneficial. For
example, if a public official has a remainder interest in a trust in
which a life estate is given to someone else who has a longer life
expectancy than the public official's, it is possible the public official
may never receive any of the trust's benefits. The Commission should
consider whether disclosure of that remote interest is practical.

The Commission should also consider whether persons who may
be entitled to remainder interests under a revocable trust (other
than the maker of the trust) should be required to disclose their
interests especially in light of the fact that a person who is entitled to remainder interests under a testamentary trust (which is
revocable until the testator dies) is not required to disclose his or
her interest.

The second suggestion relates to the reasonable diligence
which an official is required to use in determining whether the official,
spouse or dependent children have an interest, direct, indirect or
beneficial, in a trust. There is no requirement that the maker of a
trust advise the beneficiary of the existence of a trust. There is
also a question as to the diligence required for remote beneficiaries
to determine the value of their interest.

There is also the possibility that there will be duplication
in the reporting of trusts since more than one filer could have an
interest in one trust.

Finally, the staff would like guidance as to whether some
entities that are denominated as a "trust" should be excluded because
the potential for conflict of interest arising out of interests in
the trust is so small or non-existent, such as a charitable trust, a
pension trust, or a voting trust.

The staff expects to solicit more specific comments and
suggestions on these matters from persons with trust expertise prior
to the Commission's discussion of the subject.


BC:plh

sections.question_synthetic: What are the current reporting requirements for trust holdings under the Political Reform Act, and should certain types of trusts or interests be excluded from reporting?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an internal FPPC memorandum to Commission members, not a response to an advice letter request. It contains explanatory analysis and legal interpretation but does not follow the standard advice letter format with explicit QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections. Document appears truncated at the end ('First,[... truncated]'). Treated as correspondence/internal memo rather than formal advice letter.

embedding.summary: This memorandum from Barbara Campbell to FPPC Commission members outlines the current reporting requirements for trust holdings under the Political Reform Act, distinguishing between business trusts and ordinary trusts, and explaining the disclosure obligations for beneficiaries with 10% or greater interests in trusts.
